speaker: interviewer
text: Four. We are continuing our interview with Joseph Gringlas. Joseph, you talked about being in a kibbutz in Warsaw, and what was this kibbutz like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kibbutz was a-- was a lot of young kids. Some were there-- came which were saved by hiding from Gentiles, and-- but mostly it was only camp survivors, though. Some were partisans-- you know, fight against the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, mostly Jewish, and then some Gentiles came to visit us, yeah, and there was a special one, which he was a Jewish guy, and he was in the Polish army. His name was Anzek Zuckerman. He had-- he was-- he didn't look Jewish, so he went in the Polish army underground. He's the one with help in Warsaw. You hear about Warsaw was against the Germans before--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Warsaw uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Warsaw ghetto uprising, yeah. He was the head of-- in the-- [INAUDIBLE] he was giving them help with ammunition with guns-- that Anzek Zuckerman. So he's the guy who was organized the kibbutz. He was a Zionist, so he thought-- and it was young people was the organizers that kibbutz to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this kibbutz in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz was a few hundred. Probably a few hundred people. Close to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you and Sol end up going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What happened, my brother got married in kibbutz-- Sol-- and he went, and he had a child already after he got married a few years. And he emigrated. There was an opening to go to the United States, so he was-- I was there still in Germany. He was going to school. He studied electronics. I needed a profession to learn something to make a living, so I went in electronics school. Electronics school was just people from the United States, women-- they called ORT-- helping out for the people to rehabilitate to get some profession in the mid-- set up some school in electronics. My brother was married, had a kid, and was hoping to go to the United States, so he went to United States, and he went to Portland, Oregon. I stayed there yet a year more longer, then, and I-- then I was-- I wanted to go to-- it was very in my heart, was to go to Israel. It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went to the United States because your brother was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- so-- because my brother-- if-- if I got only one survivor, brother from family, I wanted to be with him, so I came to the United States in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, as I told you, he was in Portland, Oregon, and I was supposed to go to Portland, Oregon, too. But at that time, after being in Portland, Oregon, and I had some family living, and a cousin was here. So he told my brother that to make a better living, you better move from Poland to Detroit. It's a city with automobiles and manufacturing, and it was better living than in Portland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he emigrated. He came to Detroit while I was still assigned to go to Portland, Oregon. He sent a telegram to New York, its organization for new Americans, and told me to stop to Portland and to go on a train to go to Detroit. So I came to Detroit there with my brother. What was that reunion like when you saw your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a beautiful reunion. He actually [INAUDIBLE] free. He would come to a country, which helping us to make a living to start building up our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to Detroit, what did you do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there-- I was-- I still went to Cass Tech High School and continue in English in learning a profession-- electronics and television, radio television repair. And I got a job working for somebody in television and then still continuing going to school and going to classes at night to learn English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was about, I would say, about 20-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19, 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very good, but it's always you felt good [INAUDIBLE] you went through hell. You lost your family. It was-- even you're happy, but you can't be happy. Always there's a [INAUDIBLE] what you went through hell. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I lived in Detroit. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your wife here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reli.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you and Reli meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what I knew-- a friend of mine, and Reli's friend lived in that-- the friend of mine, and I'd never seen Reli there at that friend's house. But one time it was summertime. And I was-- there was a restaurant there, and there was an [INAUDIBLE] that was the whole neighborhood. I went on the [INAUDIBLE], and I was standing like at trees, [INAUDIBLE] just looking at people walking. And Ana walked by and introduced-- and she walked with the aunt, and so she introduced me. So I said, if you would be able to go-- and she's younger, much younger than me-- I would take you out for a date. And [LAUGHS] my aunt said go on. Go out for a date. We went out a few months, and then we got engaged. Start a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is Marcy, and then a few years later, we had another kid. This child is my son, Larry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my daughter is married, and I have two grandchildren. Sara is eight years old, and Jake, my grandson, is five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you continue to live in Detroit as you were raising your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, continued to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in the Jewish community in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went to Jewish community. Yeah, we always went to Jewish community to see people that we know from camps, you know. And they always have organized us to remember that for the 6 million people. So we always got together, and sometimes they made entertaining for our people, Jewish theater or something like that, concerts from Jewish center.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1992, you went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in 1992 my wife's family-- they come mostly from Czechoslovakia-- and they organized a trip to go back to see where they were born, and my wife's family mostly were hidden-- hidden by the Gentiles in Czechoslovakia in the mountains. So-- and my daughter always said she wanted to go back where I was born, in Poland. I said I didn't want to go back to Poland. I don't want to see Poland anymore, but soon they organized-- they organized a trip for my wife's family to go to Czechoslovakia. So I opened up a map, and I looked at my old town, and it hit me. I said to my daughter we're going to go to Poland for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you when you went back to your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. It was terrible. Couldn't-- couldn't find any people that I know, and I told you we went to that place where I was born. And there came out a Gentile women, and I just wanted to show my my daughter where I was born. She wouldn't let me in. She said she bought that house. I said I didn't come back for house. I come to show my kids where I was born. She didn't let me in. She didn't open the door to let us inside to show my kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then after being in in my hometown, we went to Warsaw, the capital. In Warsaw, we looked at ghettos and all the things what happened there. And from Warsaw, we went to Krakow, and Krakow-- Krakow was close to Auschwitz. We went to see where I was-- went to Auschwitz and went to Birkenau when I was there during the war at the camps. What was it like for you to go back to Birkenau after all those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what happened to me. In my home town, I was going crazy. I was terrible. I was nervous. I-- I didn't know what happened to myself, but in Auschwitz it was more like they showed-- you didn't need anybody to show you what went on. [INAUDIBLE] I went through hell, and I will tell you what went on. It was [INAUDIBLE] the barracks. We went through all these places. We saw the crematorium. What happened-- the crematorium is all ruined, because the Germans, when they fled, they bombed-- they exploded everything, but you know exactly where they were, those chimneys, all these things, where they took the people in before the crematorium, then they put them in the ovens. And as I told you before, at night in Birkenau was the sky red from burning. And this never gets out of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- what did your wife, and your daughter, and son say after seeing Birkenau and your hometown? How did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Auschwitz I remember exactly [INAUDIBLE]. My daughter-- [INAUDIBLE]-- in Auschwitz, there was some display of the people uniforms, suitcases what they brought with them, with addresses on the suitcases. They thought they're going to go back home-- and children's shoes. The worst thing is you see children's shoes, all these clothes from children, and hair, and glasses. You know what they did-- that was left of it they couldn't hide this, the Germans, so that's what they have-- everything in Auschwitz now.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- Birkenau is just about two kilometers from Auschwitz. I was never in Auschwitz. I was in Birkenau. Birkenau was the crematorium, and when I came into the-- to that building where the trains went by, I said, this was the place that I used to go through. My thought never we'll get out-- we'll get out, but some didn't. Some luck-- I don't know that-- either the people didn't-- millions of people didn't survive, but I don't know why I-- why did I survive? I guess somebody had to survive to tell what happened to us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And today it's 50-some years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 50 years, but still-- still like it was yesterday. You never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it important for you to do this interview today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the interview was-- we-- our people are getting old, and they're dying out already. This is the last witness what happened. If you even tell anybody, they wouldn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like people to learn from your story? What would you like people can take? There are so many things that you went through and so many experiences. What-- is- are there-- Are there one or two key things you'd like people to really think about and hear in your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to show what happened and the hate comes into people like a sickness. They think other people shouldn't live, and they should live. That's what shows you, and if-- if you allow this to happen-- it's still going on even now after 50 years. We still see what's going on in the world. Still coming are the sick people that want to hate, want to destroy, want to kill. It's unbelievable. People don't learn nothing, listen. But hopefully the majority will learn we should never let this happen again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my future children, grandchildren should survive and never go through what I went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to introduce my family-- my wife, Reli, my daughter, Marcy, her husband, Joel, my two grandchildren, Sara and Jake, my son, Larry, fiancee, Karen Fink, to be married October 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joseph, what's what's special today?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a special day for me to be with my family together on this holiday of Passover, and another occasion is it I was liberated in April 11, the same month. It's a big occasion for me to be seeing my daughter, and my son, and my son-in-law, and future daughter-in-law, and my wife, Reli.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and Marcy, what significance does this videotape testimony hold for you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: The videotaping is is very significant for me and for my family. Three years ago we did a-- three years ago or two years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three, four?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years ago. We did a trip-- family trek-- back to my father and my mother's hometowns, and we recorded that on our own videotape, and we had this project that we were going to record it and interview my father and my mother, so that Sarah and Jake would be able to understand and know their stories, but this videotape takes that a lot further, and not just Sarah and Jake-- although they will, of course, have this video. There will be many other people that will be able to understand and hear my father and my mother tell their own personal stories, their courageous stories, and their victory and their success in having us all together here today. I don't remember ever not knowing about the Holocaust. It seems like I was very little when I was informed about it, and now I see that my children are getting to an age to-- at least one of them-- to be able to appreciate what their-- what her grandparents went through, and today she had a special, special meeting with her grandfather, where they shared a book. And she's beginning to learn, and she would like to just say a word about how wonderful that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today me and my grandfather read a book. It's called by David Adler, and it's about the Holocaust. And it's a grandfather telling his granddaughter about the Holocaust, and the grandfather in this story went to the same concentration camp as my grandfather, so I understand a lot more, and it was special to read it with my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Larry?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess I would like to say that-- excuse me-- for many years I've been preoccupied with this question of the Holocaust, and my-- I can't do this [INAUDIBLE] and my parents. And that I've read Primo Levi, Paul Celan, and thought about some of the important historiographical issues, but, I mean, these are flat, very flat images for me. What's incredibly important and meaningful are these-- this very moment, and as Marcy said, this-- the trip four years ago to Europe was unbelievable. [SOBBING] I mean, one of the most profound moments in my life was watching my father say Kaddish in Auschwitz. I mean, it was the most moving thing in my life, and then looking for my grandmother's grave in the woods in Czechoslovakia is just-- I mean, is just beyond, I-I mean beyond words, but, I mean, obviously this is incredibly important to educate, you know, not only Jake and Sarah, hopefully her kids in the future, but that the world should know that this singular and incredible destruction of humanity-- that you know, to ensure that this doesn't happen again. And so I just applaud my parents' efforts in, you know, in educating--

speaker: interviewee
text: The world.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the world about this, you know, important event.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joseph, any final worlds?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I said it before. It's wonderful to be with my family. It makes me feel good that I'm alive and have just what I got now. It's unbelievable 50 years ago to think that I would have that. It's a gift from God that I have a family like this and be proud of them and to be-- they're hopefully proud of their parents, father, mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To have those grandchildren, wonderful grandchildren, Sarah and Jake. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And daughter, wonderful, and son-in-law, and my wife, and my son, and my future daughter-in-law. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's kissing. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: We're a kissing family, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is a picture from my older brother. He was married before the war. This was his wife, Zelda. And this is my only sister I told you that I had, Beila. The way I got this picture after the war when I came to the United States-- I learned that my-- my brother's wife had family in Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't have no address, but I knew they had something to do in food business. I went to Toronto, and I had my own cousin on my side, and I said I'm going to look for pictures-- maybe there are pictures of my-- my brother's wife's family. He said where are you going to find? You'll never find it. You don't have no address. I said, leave it up to me. If you want it, you'll find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the food market, and I remembered the last name. Her last name was [NON-ENGLISH] and I asked somebody. He said there. They're there. That's the [NON-ENGLISH]. I went there, took me home, and they had pictures from-- from my brother's wife and the picture of the wedding with my sister, [INAUDIBLE]. That was a big thing for me, because I have no pictures from other families and not from the parents. So when I found the picture, something-- something came down from sky. Something-- can't describe it. This is a picture of my brother, which he was during the war-- before the war, he went to the army, and as soon as the war broke out, they called him right away. He was in the war and fighting the Germans, in the name of the place when people came back they told us [POLISH]. That's not far from Warsaw. And the people that knew Henry came to my hometown. They said my brother is alive. He went through the war, but he's alive, but we never hear then what happened, and he's completely disappeared. This picture I got when I came to the United States. I went to Toronto, had a cousin living there before he emigrated to Canada from before the war-- the year before the war. He had a little picture of my brother from the army. So this is the picture what I found from him, and it was beautiful bit of things to say about a picture of my other brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name, again, in the picture? The picture is Jankel. And by the way, my grandson is named after him, Jake.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my brother and me after the war-- the only two of our family survived, and the picture was taken quite a few months later. And this-- the-- those uniforms used to be the pilots of the Nazis. We throw away our striped uniform and I found us the uniform, and I took all the stripes from the SS from the pilots, and it's the only uniform we had the clothes to put on. That's the picture. I am on the left side of the picture, and my brother on the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol. This is a document, which my daughter went to Washington in the Holocaust Museum, and they find these documents. And this is a document what the Germans kept-- kept all the-- from all the people were in the the camps, and when they came, what kind of work we did. It shows you 1944, and it showed here Steinbruch. That's where I worked in outside cutting the stones for the roads to build, and it's it's a German Nazi document. It's in the Washington embassy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a document [INTERPOSING VOICES] that records what kind of work went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of work we did and where it was. It was [INAUDIBLE] Poland in 1944. That was the document that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where the camp where I was. This is one of the documents which my daughter found in Washington museum-- Holocaust Museum, and it shows on the document that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when coming from-- from Blizyn to Birkenau-Auschwitz, 1,640 male Jews given the numbers B 1160 to B 2773. And the other numbers was after the A numbers. Certain people got A numbers, but I got a B number, and this is the documents that people were B number given in Auschwitz-Birkenau that came from Blizyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number is B 2247.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're in this category here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in that category [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a number tattooed on your arm here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was the number tattooed in Birkenau-Auschwitz. It was the people where they would leave for work-- to work and other people which went to gas chambers with no numbers given at all. So the one which left they thought is going to use it for work that they gave those numbers, and my number is B-2247.
